'''Niukawakami Shrine Upper Shrine''' (にうかわかみじんじゃかみしゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in Sako, Kawakami Village, Yoshino District, Nara Prefecture. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Myojin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964}}) and a {{ill|lt=claim shrine|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904}} of the {{ill|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines|Q215320|WD=Q215320}} (lower 8). Its former {{ill|lt=modern shrine rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=shrine rank}} was {{ill|lt=modern shrine rank system#Kan-sha|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Kanpei-taisha}}{{Efn2|However, that ranking was as a combined unit with the present Niukawakami Lower Shrine, or with all three including present Niukawakami Shrine (Middle Shrine).}}. Today it is a {{ill|lt=Beppyo shrine|Q10898274|WD=Q10898274}} of {{ill|lt=Jinja Honchō|Q908077|WD=Q908077}}.

The name distinguishes it from {{ill|lt=Niukawakami Shrine Lower Shrine|Q10877367|WD=Q10877367}} in {{ill|lt=Shimoichi|Q1203064|WD=Q1203064}}.

=== Enshrined deities ===
; Principal deity
* '''{{ill|lt=Okami-no-Kami|Q644201|WD=Q644201|lt=Takaokami-no-Kami}}'''

; Accompanying deities
* {{ill|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi|Q386563|WD=Q386563|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi-no-Kami}}
* Ōikazuchi-no-Kami

Previously, {{ill|lt=Mizuhanome|Q8191796|WD=Q8191796|lt=Mizuhanome-no-Kami}} was principal deity, but when merged in {{ill|lt=1922|Q2165|WD=Q2165}} (Taishō 11) with present {{ill|lt=Niukawakami Shrine|Q246370|WD=Q246370|lt=Niukawakami Shrine (Middle Shrine)}}, it was changed to Takaokami-no-Kami. Takaokami-no-Kami has long been regarded as a deity of rain and rain-stopping.

=== History ===
Until early {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}}, it was a small shrine called Takaokami Shrine, with unclear origin. Excavation with construction of {{ill|lt=Ōtaki Dam|Q11437563|WD=Q11437563}} discovered the Miyanotaira site{{Efn2|Early and late Jōmon pottery was found. Most stone tools were sinkers used in fishing nets, about 400 items.}}; directly under former main sanctuary remains were ritual-altar stones dating back before late Heian (late 11th century). Nearby were also found circular stone-arrangement remains with standing stones, seen as ritual remains from end of middle to early late {{ill|lt=Jōmon period|Q52824|WD=Q52824}} (about 4,000 years ago). Though a break is recognized through Nara and Kofun periods, shrine ritual space may trace back to Jōmon.

In {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}} it was ranked {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Gōsha|1=UNKNOWN}}. But Etō Masazumi, assistant priest of then-Kanpei-taisha {{ill|lt=Niukawakami Shrine Lower Shrine|Q10877367|WD=Q10877367|lt=Niukawakami Shrine}} (present lower shrine), argued lower shrine location did not match the four-boundary area in the 895 Daijōkan decree ('{{ill|lt=Ruijū Sandai Kyaku|Q11665185|WD=Q11665185}}'), identifying this shrine as the Engishiki Niukawakami Shrine. In 1874 this shrine became under lower shrine jurisdiction; lower shrine called “Kuchi-no-miya” and this shrine “Oku-no-miya.” After Etō’s view was accepted, in {{ill|lt=1896|Q7846|WD=Q7846}} “Kuchi-no-miya” was renamed “Niukawakami Lower Shrine,” this shrine “Upper Shrine,” and both together became “Kanpei-taisha Niukawakami Shrine.”

However, in {{ill|lt=1915|Q2084|WD=Q2084}} (Taishō 4), Moriguchi Narakichi of Higashiyoshino (site of present middle shrine) published 'Study of Niukawakami Shrine,' arguing “Aritōri Shrine (present middle shrine) = Niukawakami.” This was accepted, and by Interior Ministry notice of Oct. 12, 1922, middle shrine was designated; upper and lower shrines were incorporated into it, and all three again treated together as “Kanpei-taisha Niukawakami Shrine.” At that time, upper shrine deity changed back from Mizuhanome-no-Kami to Takaokami-no-Kami, as in its Gōsha period.

After World War II, in {{ill|lt=1952|Q5272|WD=Q5272}} it became independent. It now belongs to {{ill|lt=Jinja Honchō|Q908077|WD=Q908077}} and is a {{ill|lt=Beppyo shrine|Q10898274|WD=Q10898274}}.

=== Precincts ===
Current main sanctuary is three-bay nagare-zukuri with copper roof. As old precinct area was to be submerged by Ōtaki Dam construction, it was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1998|Q2089|WD=Q2089}} using old timber from former shrine buildings of {{ill|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|Q687168|WD=Q687168}}. The old shrine buildings were built in {{ill|lt=1917|Q2092|WD=Q2092}} and relocated to {{ill|lt=Asukaniimasu Shrine|Q11665959|WD=Q11665959}} (Asuka Village), becoming that shrine’s main sanctuary etc.
<gallery>
File:Niukawakami-jinja Kamisha Haiden（Worship Hall） 001.jpg|Worship hall
File:Niukawakami-jinja Kamisha, honden.jpg|Main sanctuary
File:Niukawakami-jinja Kamisha, saishi-ikou.jpg|Ritual remains (restored)
File:Niukawakami-jinja Kamisha Keidai 001.jpg|View from area in front of worship hall
</gallery>

=== Sessha and massha ===
* Atago-sha ({{ill|lt=Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|WD=Q1640658|lt=Homusubi-no-Kami}})
* Ebisu-sha ({{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Kami}} / {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}})
* Suijin-sha (Mitsuhanome-no-Mikoto)
* Yama-no-kami-sha ({{ill|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi|Q386563|WD=Q386563|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi-no-Kami}})

=== Main festivals ===
* Annual festival ({{ill|lt=October 8|Q2924|WD=Q2924}})

=== Cultural property ===

{{nihongo|'''Niukawakami Shrine Kamisha'''|丹生川上神社上社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Yamato Province]].

== instance of (P31) ==

* {{ill|Shinto shrine|lt=Shinto shrine|ar|جينجا|arz|ضريح شنتو|bcl|Shinto shrine|ca|Jinja|cs|Šintoistická svatyně|da|Shinto-tempel|de|Shintō-Schrein|el|Σιντοϊστικό Ιερό|en|Shinto shrine|eo|Ŝintoa sanktejo|es|Jinja|eu|Jinja|fa|معبد شینتویی|fi|Šintolainen pyhäkkö|fr|Sanctuaire shinto|hi|शिंतो मंदिर|hr|Šintoistički hram|hu|Sintó szentély|id|Jinja|it|Jinja|ja|神社|ka|ძინძია|ko|신사 (신토)|lb|Shintō-Schräin|mr|शिंतो मंदिर|nl|Jinja (heiligdom)|no|Jinja|or|ଜିଞ୍ଜା|pl|Jinja|pt|Templo xintoísta|ru|Дзиндзя|sc|Jinja|simple|Shinto shrine|sl|Šintoistično svetišče|sr|Шинтоистички храм|sv|Jinja|th|ศาลเจ้าชินโต|tr|Şinto tapınağı|uk|Святилище (синто)|vi|Thần xã|wuu|神社|zh_yue|神社|zh|神社|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}}

'''Niukawakami Shrine Upper Shrine''' (にうかわかみじんじゃかみしゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Sako, Kawakami Village, Yoshino District, Nara Prefecture. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Myojin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}) and a {{ill|lt=claim shrine|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}} of the {{ill|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines|Q215320|WD=Q215320|1=Twenty-Two Shrines}} (lower 8). Its former {{ill|lt=modern shrine rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was {{ill|lt=modern shrine rank system#Kan-sha|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Kanpei-taisha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}{{Efn2|However, that ranking was as a combined unit with the present Niukawakami Lower Shrine, or with all three including present Niukawakami Shrine (Middle Shrine).}}. Today it is a {{ill|lt=Beppyo shrine|Q10898274|WD=Q10898274|1=Beppyo shrine}} of {{ill|lt=Jinja Honchō|Q908077|WD=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}}.

The name distinguishes it from {{ill|lt=Niukawakami Shrine Lower Shrine|Q10877367|WD=Q10877367|1=Niukawakami Lower Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Shimoichi|Q1203064|WD=Q1203064|1=Shimoichi, Nara}}.

=== Enshrined deities ===
; Principal deity
* '''{{ill|lt=Okami-no-Kami|Q644201|WD=Q644201|lt=Takaokami-no-Kami|1=Kuraokami}}'''

; Accompanying deities
* {{ill|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi|Q386563|WD=Q386563|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi-no-Kami|1=Ōyamatsumi}}
* Ōikazuchi-no-Kami

Previously, {{ill|lt=Mizuhanome|Q8191796|WD=Q8191796|lt=Mizuhanome-no-Kami|1=Mizuhanome}} was principal deity, but when merged in {{ill|lt=1922|Q2165|WD=Q2165|1=1922}} (Taishō 11) with present {{ill|lt=Niukawakami Shrine|Q246370|WD=Q246370|lt=Niukawakami Shrine (Middle Shrine)|1=Niukawakami Shrine (Q246370)}}, it was changed to Takaokami-no-Kami. Takaokami-no-Kami has long been regarded as a deity of rain and rain-stopping.

=== History ===
Until early {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}, it was a small shrine called Takaokami Shrine, with unclear origin. Excavation with construction of {{ill|lt=Ōtaki Dam|Q11437563|WD=Q11437563|1=Ohtaki Dam}} discovered the Miyanotaira site{{Efn2|Early and late Jōmon pottery was found. Most stone tools were sinkers used in fishing nets, about 400 items.}}; directly under former main sanctuary remains were ritual-altar stones dating back before late Heian (late 11th century). Nearby were also found circular stone-arrangement remains with standing stones, seen as ritual remains from end of middle to early late {{ill|lt=Jōmon period|Q52824|WD=Q52824|1=Jōmon period}} (about 4,000 years ago). Though a break is recognized through Nara and Kofun periods, shrine ritual space may trace back to Jōmon.

In {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}} it was ranked {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Gōsha|1=UNKNOWN}}. But Etō Masazumi, assistant priest of then-Kanpei-taisha {{ill|lt=Niukawakami Shrine Lower Shrine|Q10877367|WD=Q10877367|lt=Niukawakami Shrine|1=Niukawakami Lower Shrine}} (present lower shrine), argued lower shrine location did not match the four-boundary area in the 895 Daijōkan decree ('{{ill|lt=Ruijū Sandai Kyaku|Q11665185|WD=Q11665185|1=Ruijū Sandai Kyaku}}'), identifying this shrine as the Engishiki Niukawakami Shrine. In 1874 this shrine became under lower shrine jurisdiction; lower shrine called “Kuchi-no-miya” and this shrine “Oku-no-miya.” After Etō’s view was accepted, in {{ill|lt=1896|Q7846|WD=Q7846|1=1896}} “Kuchi-no-miya” was renamed “Niukawakami Lower Shrine,” this shrine “Upper Shrine,” and both together became “Kanpei-taisha Niukawakami Shrine.”

However, in {{ill|lt=1915|Q2084|WD=Q2084|1=1915}} (Taishō 4), Moriguchi Narakichi of Higashiyoshino (site of present middle shrine) published 'Study of Niukawakami Shrine,' arguing “Aritōri Shrine (present middle shrine) = Niukawakami.” This was accepted, and by Interior Ministry notice of Oct. 12, 1922, middle shrine was designated; upper and lower shrines were incorporated into it, and all three again treated together as “Kanpei-taisha Niukawakami Shrine.” At that time, upper shrine deity changed back from Mizuhanome-no-Kami to Takaokami-no-Kami, as in its Gōsha period.

After World War II, in {{ill|lt=1952|Q5272|WD=Q5272|1=1952}} it became independent. It now belongs to {{ill|lt=Jinja Honchō|Q908077|WD=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}} and is a {{ill|lt=Beppyo shrine|Q10898274|WD=Q10898274|1=Beppyo shrine}}.

=== Precincts ===
Current main sanctuary is three-bay nagare-zukuri with copper roof. As old precinct area was to be submerged by Ōtaki Dam construction, it was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1998|Q2089|WD=Q2089|1=1998}} using old timber from former shrine buildings of {{ill|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|Q687168|WD=Q687168|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}. The old shrine buildings were built in {{ill|lt=1917|Q2092|WD=Q2092|1=1917}} and relocated to {{ill|lt=Asukaniimasu Shrine|Q11665959|WD=Q11665959|1=Asuka-ni-Imasu Shrine}} (Asuka Village), becoming that shrine’s main sanctuary etc.

{{nihongo|'''Niukawakami Shrine Kamisha'''|丹生川上神社上社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Yamato Province]].

'''Niukawakami Shrine Upper Shrine''' (にうかわかみじんじゃかみしゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Sako, Kawakami Village, Yoshino District, Nara Prefecture. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Myojin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}) and a {{ill|lt=claim shrine|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}} of the {{ill|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines|Q215320|WD=Q215320|1=Twenty-Two Shrines}} (lower 8). Its former {{ill|lt=modern shrine rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was {{ill|lt=modern shrine rank system#Kan-sha|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Kanpei-taisha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}{{Efn2|However, that ranking was as a combined unit with the present Niukawakami Lower Shrine, or with all three including present Niukawakami Shrine (Middle Shrine).}}. Today it is a {{ill|lt=Beppyo shrine|Q10898274|WD=Q10898274|1=Beppyo shrine}} of {{ill|lt=Jinja Honchō|Q908077|WD=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}}.

The name distinguishes it from {{ill|lt=Niukawakami Shrine Lower Shrine|Q10877367|WD=Q10877367|1=Niukawakami Lower Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Shimoichi|Q1203064|WD=Q1203064|1=Shimoichi, Nara}}.

=== Enshrined deities ===
; Principal deity
* '''{{ill|lt=Okami-no-Kami|Q644201|WD=Q644201|lt=Takaokami-no-Kami|1=Kuraokami}}'''

; Accompanying deities
* {{ill|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi|Q386563|WD=Q386563|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi-no-Kami|1=Ōyamatsumi}}
* Ōikazuchi-no-Kami

Previously, {{ill|lt=Mizuhanome|Q8191796|WD=Q8191796|lt=Mizuhanome-no-Kami|1=Mizuhanome}} was principal deity, but when merged in {{ill|lt=1922|Q2165|WD=Q2165|1=1922}} (Taishō 11) with present {{ill|lt=Niukawakami Shrine|Q246370|WD=Q246370|lt=Niukawakami Shrine (Middle Shrine)|1=Niukawakami Shrine (Q246370)}}, it was changed to Takaokami-no-Kami. Takaokami-no-Kami has long been regarded as a deity of rain and rain-stopping.

=== History ===
Until early {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}, it was a small shrine called Takaokami Shrine, with unclear origin. Excavation with construction of {{ill|lt=Ōtaki Dam|Q11437563|WD=Q11437563|1=Ohtaki Dam}} discovered the Miyanotaira site{{Efn2|Early and late Jōmon pottery was found. Most stone tools were sinkers used in fishing nets, about 400 items.}}; directly under former main sanctuary remains were ritual-altar stones dating back before late Heian (late 11th century). Nearby were also found circular stone-arrangement remains with standing stones, seen as ritual remains from end of middle to early late {{ill|lt=Jōmon period|Q52824|WD=Q52824|1=Jōmon period}} (about 4,000 years ago). Though a break is recognized through Nara and Kofun periods, shrine ritual space may trace back to Jōmon.

In {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}} it was ranked {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Gōsha|1=UNKNOWN}}. But Etō Masazumi, assistant priest of then-Kanpei-taisha {{ill|lt=Niukawakami Shrine Lower Shrine|Q10877367|WD=Q10877367|lt=Niukawakami Shrine|1=Niukawakami Lower Shrine}} (present lower shrine), argued lower shrine location did not match the four-boundary area in the 895 Daijōkan decree ('{{ill|lt=Ruijū Sandai Kyaku|Q11665185|WD=Q11665185|1=Ruijū Sandai Kyaku}}'), identifying this shrine as the Engishiki Niukawakami Shrine. In 1874 this shrine became under lower shrine jurisdiction; lower shrine called “Kuchi-no-miya” and this shrine “Oku-no-miya.” After Etō’s view was accepted, in {{ill|lt=1896|Q7846|WD=Q7846|1=1896}} “Kuchi-no-miya” was renamed “Niukawakami Lower Shrine,” this shrine “Upper Shrine,” and both together became “Kanpei-taisha Niukawakami Shrine.”

However, in {{ill|lt=1915|Q2084|WD=Q2084|1=1915}} (Taishō 4), Moriguchi Narakichi of Higashiyoshino (site of present middle shrine) published 'Study of Niukawakami Shrine,' arguing “Aritōri Shrine (present middle shrine) = Niukawakami.” This was accepted, and by Interior Ministry notice of Oct. 12, 1922, middle shrine was designated; upper and lower shrines were incorporated into it, and all three again treated together as “Kanpei-taisha Niukawakami Shrine.” At that time, upper shrine deity changed back from Mizuhanome-no-Kami to Takaokami-no-Kami, as in its Gōsha period.

After World War II, in {{ill|lt=1952|Q5272|WD=Q5272|1=1952}} it became independent. It now belongs to {{ill|lt=Jinja Honchō|Q908077|WD=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}} and is a {{ill|lt=Beppyo shrine|Q10898274|WD=Q10898274|1=Beppyo shrine}}.

=== Precincts ===
Current main sanctuary is three-bay nagare-zukuri with copper roof. As old precinct area was to be submerged by Ōtaki Dam construction, it was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1998|Q2089|WD=Q2089|1=1998}} using old timber from former shrine buildings of {{ill|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|Q687168|WD=Q687168|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}. The old shrine buildings were built in {{ill|lt=1917|Q2092|WD=Q2092|1=1917}} and relocated to {{ill|lt=Asukaniimasu Shrine|Q11665959|WD=Q11665959|1=Asuka-ni-Imasu Shrine}} (Asuka Village), becoming that shrine’s main sanctuary etc.

{{nihongo|'''Niukawakami Shrine Kamisha'''|丹生川上神社上社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Yamato Province]].

'''Niukawakami Shrine Upper Shrine''' (にうかわかみじんじゃかみしゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Sako, Kawakami Village, Yoshino District, Nara Prefecture. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Myojin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}) and a {{ill|lt=claim shrine|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}} of the {{ill|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines|Q215320|WD=Q215320|1=Twenty-Two Shrines}} (lower 8). Its former {{ill|lt=modern shrine rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was {{ill|lt=modern shrine rank system#Kan-sha|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Kanpei-taisha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}{{Efn2|However, that ranking was as a combined unit with the present Niukawakami Lower Shrine, or with all three including present Niukawakami Shrine (Middle Shrine).}}. Today it is a {{ill|lt=Beppyo shrine|Q10898274|WD=Q10898274|1=Beppyo shrine}} of {{ill|lt=Jinja Honchō|Q908077|WD=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}}.

The name distinguishes it from {{ill|lt=Niukawakami Shrine Lower Shrine|Q10877367|WD=Q10877367|1=Niukawakami Lower Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Shimoichi|Q1203064|WD=Q1203064|1=Shimoichi, Nara}}.

=== Enshrined deities ===
; Principal deity
* '''{{ill|lt=Okami-no-Kami|Q644201|WD=Q644201|lt=Takaokami-no-Kami|1=Kuraokami}}'''

; Accompanying deities
* {{ill|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi|Q386563|WD=Q386563|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi-no-Kami|1=Ōyamatsumi}}
* Ōikazuchi-no-Kami

Previously, {{ill|lt=Mizuhanome|Q8191796|WD=Q8191796|lt=Mizuhanome-no-Kami|1=Mizuhanome}} was principal deity, but when merged in {{ill|lt=1922|Q2165|WD=Q2165|1=1922}} (Taishō 11) with present {{ill|lt=Niukawakami Shrine|Q246370|WD=Q246370|lt=Niukawakami Shrine (Middle Shrine)|1=Niukawakami Shrine (Q246370)}}, it was changed to Takaokami-no-Kami. Takaokami-no-Kami has long been regarded as a deity of rain and rain-stopping.

=== History ===
Until early {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}, it was a small shrine called Takaokami Shrine, with unclear origin. Excavation with construction of {{ill|lt=Ōtaki Dam|Q11437563|WD=Q11437563|1=Ohtaki Dam}} discovered the Miyanotaira site{{Efn2|Early and late Jōmon pottery was found. Most stone tools were sinkers used in fishing nets, about 400 items.}}; directly under former main sanctuary remains were ritual-altar stones dating back before late Heian (late 11th century). Nearby were also found circular stone-arrangement remains with standing stones, seen as ritual remains from end of middle to early late {{ill|lt=Jōmon period|Q52824|WD=Q52824|1=Jōmon period}} (about 4,000 years ago). Though a break is recognized through Nara and Kofun periods, shrine ritual space may trace back to Jōmon.

In {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}} it was ranked {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Gōsha|1=UNKNOWN}}. But Etō Masazumi, assistant priest of then-Kanpei-taisha {{ill|lt=Niukawakami Shrine Lower Shrine|Q10877367|WD=Q10877367|lt=Niukawakami Shrine|1=Niukawakami Lower Shrine}} (present lower shrine), argued lower shrine location did not match the four-boundary area in the 895 Daijōkan decree ('{{ill|lt=Ruijū Sandai Kyaku|Q11665185|WD=Q11665185|1=Ruijū Sandai Kyaku}}'), identifying this shrine as the Engishiki Niukawakami Shrine. In 1874 this shrine became under lower shrine jurisdiction; lower shrine called “Kuchi-no-miya” and this shrine “Oku-no-miya.” After Etō’s view was accepted, in {{ill|lt=1896|Q7846|WD=Q7846|1=1896}} “Kuchi-no-miya” was renamed “Niukawakami Lower Shrine,” this shrine “Upper Shrine,” and both together became “Kanpei-taisha Niukawakami Shrine.”

However, in {{ill|lt=1915|Q2084|WD=Q2084|1=1915}} (Taishō 4), Moriguchi Narakichi of Higashiyoshino (site of present middle shrine) published 'Study of Niukawakami Shrine,' arguing “Aritōri Shrine (present middle shrine) = Niukawakami.” This was accepted, and by Interior Ministry notice of Oct. 12, 1922, middle shrine was designated; upper and lower shrines were incorporated into it, and all three again treated together as “Kanpei-taisha Niukawakami Shrine.” At that time, upper shrine deity changed back from Mizuhanome-no-Kami to Takaokami-no-Kami, as in its Gōsha period.

After World War II, in {{ill|lt=1952|Q5272|WD=Q5272|1=1952}} it became independent. It now belongs to {{ill|lt=Jinja Honchō|Q908077|WD=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}} and is a {{ill|lt=Beppyo shrine|Q10898274|WD=Q10898274|1=Beppyo shrine}}.

=== Precincts ===
Current main sanctuary is three-bay nagare-zukuri with copper roof. As old precinct area was to be submerged by Ōtaki Dam construction, it was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1998|Q2089|WD=Q2089|1=1998}} using old timber from former shrine buildings of {{ill|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|Q687168|WD=Q687168|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}. The old shrine buildings were built in {{ill|lt=1917|Q2092|WD=Q2092|1=1917}} and relocated to {{ill|lt=Asukaniimasu Shrine|Q11665959|WD=Q11665959|1=Asuka-ni-Imasu Shrine}} (Asuka Village), becoming that shrine’s main sanctuary etc.

=== Sessha and massha ===
* Atago-sha ({{ill|lt=Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|WD=Q1640658|lt=Homusubi-no-Kami|1=Kagu-tsuchi}})

{{nihongo|'''Niukawakami Shrine Kamisha'''|丹生川上神社上社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Yamato Province]].

'''Niukawakami Shrine Upper Shrine''' (にうかわかみじんじゃかみしゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Sako, Kawakami Village, Yoshino District, Nara Prefecture. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Myojin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}) and a {{ill|lt=claim shrine|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}} of the {{ill|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines|Q215320|WD=Q215320|1=Twenty-Two Shrines}} (lower 8). Its former {{ill|lt=modern shrine rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was {{ill|lt=modern shrine rank system#Kan-sha|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Kanpei-taisha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}{{Efn2|However, that ranking was as a combined unit with the present Niukawakami Lower Shrine, or with all three including present Niukawakami Shrine (Middle Shrine).}}. Today it is a {{ill|lt=Beppyo shrine|Q10898274|WD=Q10898274|1=Beppyo shrine}} of {{ill|lt=Jinja Honchō|Q908077|WD=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}}.

The name distinguishes it from {{ill|lt=Niukawakami Shrine Lower Shrine|Q10877367|WD=Q10877367|1=Niukawakami Lower Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Shimoichi|Q1203064|WD=Q1203064|1=Shimoichi, Nara}}.

=== Enshrined deities ===
; Principal deity
* '''{{ill|lt=Okami-no-Kami|Q644201|WD=Q644201|lt=Takaokami-no-Kami|1=Kuraokami}}'''

; Accompanying deities
* {{ill|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi|Q386563|WD=Q386563|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi-no-Kami|1=Ōyamatsumi}}
* Ōikazuchi-no-Kami

Previously, {{ill|lt=Mizuhanome|Q8191796|WD=Q8191796|lt=Mizuhanome-no-Kami|1=Mizuhanome}} was principal deity, but when merged in {{ill|lt=1922|Q2165|WD=Q2165|1=1922}} (Taishō 11) with present {{ill|lt=Niukawakami Shrine|Q246370|WD=Q246370|lt=Niukawakami Shrine (Middle Shrine)|1=Niukawakami Shrine (Q246370)}}, it was changed to Takaokami-no-Kami. Takaokami-no-Kami has long been regarded as a deity of rain and rain-stopping.

=== History ===
Until early {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}, it was a small shrine called Takaokami Shrine, with unclear origin. Excavation with construction of {{ill|lt=Ōtaki Dam|Q11437563|WD=Q11437563|1=Ohtaki Dam}} discovered the Miyanotaira site{{Efn2|Early and late Jōmon pottery was found. Most stone tools were sinkers used in fishing nets, about 400 items.}}; directly under former main sanctuary remains were ritual-altar stones dating back before late Heian (late 11th century). Nearby were also found circular stone-arrangement remains with standing stones, seen as ritual remains from end of middle to early late {{ill|lt=Jōmon period|Q52824|WD=Q52824|1=Jōmon period}} (about 4,000 years ago). Though a break is recognized through Nara and Kofun periods, shrine ritual space may trace back to Jōmon.

In {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}} it was ranked {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Gōsha|1=UNKNOWN}}. But Etō Masazumi, assistant priest of then-Kanpei-taisha {{ill|lt=Niukawakami Shrine Lower Shrine|Q10877367|WD=Q10877367|lt=Niukawakami Shrine|1=Niukawakami Lower Shrine}} (present lower shrine), argued lower shrine location did not match the four-boundary area in the 895 Daijōkan decree ('{{ill|lt=Ruijū Sandai Kyaku|Q11665185|WD=Q11665185|1=Ruijū Sandai Kyaku}}'), identifying this shrine as the Engishiki Niukawakami Shrine. In 1874 this shrine became under lower shrine jurisdiction; lower shrine called “Kuchi-no-miya” and this shrine “Oku-no-miya.” After Etō’s view was accepted, in {{ill|lt=1896|Q7846|WD=Q7846|1=1896}} “Kuchi-no-miya” was renamed “Niukawakami Lower Shrine,” this shrine “Upper Shrine,” and both together became “Kanpei-taisha Niukawakami Shrine.”

However, in {{ill|lt=1915|Q2084|WD=Q2084|1=1915}} (Taishō 4), Moriguchi Narakichi of Higashiyoshino (site of present middle shrine) published 'Study of Niukawakami Shrine,' arguing “Aritōri Shrine (present middle shrine) = Niukawakami.” This was accepted, and by Interior Ministry notice of Oct. 12, 1922, middle shrine was designated; upper and lower shrines were incorporated into it, and all three again treated together as “Kanpei-taisha Niukawakami Shrine.” At that time, upper shrine deity changed back from Mizuhanome-no-Kami to Takaokami-no-Kami, as in its Gōsha period.

After World War II, in {{ill|lt=1952|Q5272|WD=Q5272|1=1952}} it became independent. It now belongs to {{ill|lt=Jinja Honchō|Q908077|WD=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}} and is a {{ill|lt=Beppyo shrine|Q10898274|WD=Q10898274|1=Beppyo shrine}}.

=== Precincts ===
Current main sanctuary is three-bay nagare-zukuri with copper roof. As old precinct area was to be submerged by Ōtaki Dam construction, it was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1998|Q2089|WD=Q2089|1=1998}} using old timber from former shrine buildings of {{ill|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|Q687168|WD=Q687168|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}. The old shrine buildings were built in {{ill|lt=1917|Q2092|WD=Q2092|1=1917}} and relocated to {{ill|lt=Asukaniimasu Shrine|Q11665959|WD=Q11665959|1=Asuka-ni-Imasu Shrine}} (Asuka Village), becoming that shrine’s main sanctuary etc.

{{nihongo|'''Niukawakami Shrine Kamisha'''|丹生川上神社上社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Yamato Province]].

'''Niukawakami Shrine Upper Shrine''' (にうかわかみじんじゃかみしゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Sako, Kawakami Village, Yoshino District, Nara Prefecture. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Myojin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}) and a {{ill|lt=claim shrine|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}} of the {{ill|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines|Q215320|WD=Q215320|1=Twenty-Two Shrines}} (lower 8). Its former {{ill|lt=modern shrine rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was {{ill|lt=modern shrine rank system#Kan-sha|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Kanpei-taisha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}{{Efn2|However, that ranking was as a combined unit with the present Niukawakami Lower Shrine, or with all three including present Niukawakami Shrine (Middle Shrine).}}. Today it is a {{ill|lt=Beppyo shrine|Q10898274|WD=Q10898274|1=Beppyo shrine}} of {{ill|lt=Jinja Honchō|Q908077|WD=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}}.

The name distinguishes it from {{ill|lt=Niukawakami Shrine Lower Shrine|Q10877367|WD=Q10877367|1=Niukawakami Lower Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Shimoichi|Q1203064|WD=Q1203064|1=Shimoichi, Nara}}.

=== Enshrined deities ===
; Principal deity
* '''{{ill|lt=Okami-no-Kami|Q644201|WD=Q644201|lt=Takaokami-no-Kami|1=Kuraokami}}'''

; Accompanying deities
* {{ill|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi|Q386563|WD=Q386563|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi-no-Kami|1=Ōyamatsumi}}
* Ōikazuchi-no-Kami

Previously, {{ill|lt=Mizuhanome|Q8191796|WD=Q8191796|lt=Mizuhanome-no-Kami|1=Mizuhanome}} was principal deity, but when merged in {{ill|lt=1922|Q2165|WD=Q2165|1=1922}} (Taishō 11) with present {{ill|lt=Niukawakami Shrine|Q246370|WD=Q246370|lt=Niukawakami Shrine (Middle Shrine)|1=Niukawakami Shrine (Q246370)}}, it was changed to Takaokami-no-Kami. Takaokami-no-Kami has long been regarded as a deity of rain and rain-stopping.

=== History ===
Until early {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}, it was a small shrine called Takaokami Shrine, with unclear origin. Excavation with construction of {{ill|lt=Ōtaki Dam|Q11437563|WD=Q11437563|1=Ohtaki Dam}} discovered the Miyanotaira site{{Efn2|Early and late Jōmon pottery was found. Most stone tools were sinkers used in fishing nets, about 400 items.}}; directly under former main sanctuary remains were ritual-altar stones dating back before late Heian (late 11th century). Nearby were also found circular stone-arrangement remains with standing stones, seen as ritual remains from end of middle to early late {{ill|lt=Jōmon period|Q52824|WD=Q52824|1=Jōmon period}} (about 4,000 years ago). Though a break is recognized through Nara and Kofun periods, shrine ritual space may trace back to Jōmon.

In {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}} it was ranked {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Gōsha|1=UNKNOWN}}. But Etō Masazumi, assistant priest of then-Kanpei-taisha {{ill|lt=Niukawakami Shrine Lower Shrine|Q10877367|WD=Q10877367|lt=Niukawakami Shrine|1=Niukawakami Lower Shrine}} (present lower shrine), argued lower shrine location did not match the four-boundary area in the 895 Daijōkan decree ('{{ill|lt=Ruijū Sandai Kyaku|Q11665185|WD=Q11665185|1=Ruijū Sandai Kyaku}}'), identifying this shrine as the Engishiki Niukawakami Shrine. In 1874 this shrine became under lower shrine jurisdiction; lower shrine called “Kuchi-no-miya” and this shrine “Oku-no-miya.” After Etō’s view was accepted, in {{ill|lt=1896|Q7846|WD=Q7846|1=1896}} “Kuchi-no-miya” was renamed “Niukawakami Lower Shrine,” this shrine “Upper Shrine,” and both together became “Kanpei-taisha Niukawakami Shrine.”

However, in {{ill|lt=1915|Q2084|WD=Q2084|1=1915}} (Taishō 4), Moriguchi Narakichi of Higashiyoshino (site of present middle shrine) published 'Study of Niukawakami Shrine,' arguing “Aritōri Shrine (present middle shrine) = Niukawakami.” This was accepted, and by Interior Ministry notice of Oct. 12, 1922, middle shrine was designated; upper and lower shrines were incorporated into it, and all three again treated together as “Kanpei-taisha Niukawakami Shrine.” At that time, upper shrine deity changed back from Mizuhanome-no-Kami to Takaokami-no-Kami, as in its Gōsha period.

After World War II, in {{ill|lt=1952|Q5272|WD=Q5272|1=1952}} it became independent. It now belongs to {{ill|lt=Jinja Honchō|Q908077|WD=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}} and is a {{ill|lt=Beppyo shrine|Q10898274|WD=Q10898274|1=Beppyo shrine}}.

=== Precincts ===
Current main sanctuary is three-bay nagare-zukuri with copper roof. As old precinct area was to be submerged by Ōtaki Dam construction, it was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1998|Q2089|WD=Q2089|1=1998}} using old timber from former shrine buildings of {{ill|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|Q687168|WD=Q687168|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}. The old shrine buildings were built in {{ill|lt=1917|Q2092|WD=Q2092|1=1917}} and relocated to {{ill|lt=Asukaniimasu Shrine|Q11665959|WD=Q11665959|1=Asuka-ni-Imasu Shrine}} (Asuka Village), becoming that shrine’s main sanctuary etc.

{{nihongo|'''Niukawakami Shrine Kamisha'''|丹生川上神社上社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Yamato Province]].

'''Niukawakami Shrine Upper Shrine''' (にうかわかみじんじゃかみしゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Sako, Kawakami Village, Yoshino District, Nara Prefecture. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Myojin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}) and a {{ill|lt=claim shrine|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}} of the {{ill|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines|Q215320|WD=Q215320|1=Twenty-Two Shrines}} (lower 8). Its former {{ill|lt=modern shrine rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was {{ill|lt=modern shrine rank system#Kan-sha|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Kanpei-taisha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}{{Efn2|However, that ranking was as a combined unit with the present Niukawakami Lower Shrine, or with all three including present Niukawakami Shrine (Middle Shrine).}}. Today it is a {{ill|lt=Beppyo shrine|Q10898274|WD=Q10898274|1=Beppyo shrine}} of {{ill|lt=Jinja Honchō|Q908077|WD=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}}.

The name distinguishes it from {{ill|lt=Niukawakami Shrine Lower Shrine|Q10877367|WD=Q10877367|1=Niukawakami Lower Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Shimoichi|Q1203064|WD=Q1203064|1=Shimoichi, Nara}}.

=== Enshrined deities ===
; Principal deity
* '''{{ill|lt=Okami-no-Kami|Q644201|WD=Q644201|lt=Takaokami-no-Kami|1=Kuraokami}}'''

; Accompanying deities
* {{ill|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi|Q386563|WD=Q386563|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi-no-Kami|1=Ōyamatsumi}}
* Ōikazuchi-no-Kami

Previously, {{ill|lt=Mizuhanome|Q8191796|WD=Q8191796|lt=Mizuhanome-no-Kami|1=Mizuhanome}} was principal deity, but when merged in {{ill|lt=1922|Q2165|WD=Q2165|1=1922}} (Taishō 11) with present {{ill|lt=Niukawakami Shrine|Q246370|WD=Q246370|lt=Niukawakami Shrine (Middle Shrine)|1=Niukawakami Shrine (Q246370)}}, it was changed to Takaokami-no-Kami. Takaokami-no-Kami has long been regarded as a deity of rain and rain-stopping.

=== History ===
Until early {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}, it was a small shrine called Takaokami Shrine, with unclear origin. Excavation with construction of {{ill|lt=Ōtaki Dam|Q11437563|WD=Q11437563|1=Ohtaki Dam}} discovered the Miyanotaira site{{Efn2|Early and late Jōmon pottery was found. Most stone tools were sinkers used in fishing nets, about 400 items.}}; directly under former main sanctuary remains were ritual-altar stones dating back before late Heian (late 11th century). Nearby were also found circular stone-arrangement remains with standing stones, seen as ritual remains from end of middle to early late {{ill|lt=Jōmon period|Q52824|WD=Q52824|1=Jōmon period}} (about 4,000 years ago). Though a break is recognized through Nara and Kofun periods, shrine ritual space may trace back to Jōmon.

In {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}} it was ranked {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Gōsha|1=UNKNOWN}}. But Etō Masazumi, assistant priest of then-Kanpei-taisha {{ill|lt=Niukawakami Shrine Lower Shrine|Q10877367|WD=Q10877367|lt=Niukawakami Shrine|1=Niukawakami Lower Shrine}} (present lower shrine), argued lower shrine location did not match the four-boundary area in the 895 Daijōkan decree ('{{ill|lt=Ruijū Sandai Kyaku|Q11665185|WD=Q11665185|1=Ruijū Sandai Kyaku}}'), identifying this shrine as the Engishiki Niukawakami Shrine. In 1874 this shrine became under lower shrine jurisdiction; lower shrine called “Kuchi-no-miya” and this shrine “Oku-no-miya.” After Etō’s view was accepted, in {{ill|lt=1896|Q7846|WD=Q7846|1=1896}} “Kuchi-no-miya” was renamed “Niukawakami Lower Shrine,” this shrine “Upper Shrine,” and both together became “Kanpei-taisha Niukawakami Shrine.”

However, in {{ill|lt=1915|Q2084|WD=Q2084|1=1915}} (Taishō 4), Moriguchi Narakichi of Higashiyoshino (site of present middle shrine) published 'Study of Niukawakami Shrine,' arguing “Aritōri Shrine (present middle shrine) = Niukawakami.” This was accepted, and by Interior Ministry notice of Oct. 12, 1922, middle shrine was designated; upper and lower shrines were incorporated into it, and all three again treated together as “Kanpei-taisha Niukawakami Shrine.” At that time, upper shrine deity changed back from Mizuhanome-no-Kami to Takaokami-no-Kami, as in its Gōsha period.

After World War II, in {{ill|lt=1952|Q5272|WD=Q5272|1=1952}} it became independent. It now belongs to {{ill|lt=Jinja Honchō|Q908077|WD=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}} and is a {{ill|lt=Beppyo shrine|Q10898274|WD=Q10898274|1=Beppyo shrine}}.

=== Precincts ===
Current main sanctuary is three-bay nagare-zukuri with copper roof. As old precinct area was to be submerged by Ōtaki Dam construction, it was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1998|Q2089|WD=Q2089|1=1998}} using old timber from former shrine buildings of {{ill|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|Q687168|WD=Q687168|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}. The old shrine buildings were built in {{ill|lt=1917|Q2092|WD=Q2092|1=1917}} and relocated to {{ill|lt=Asukaniimasu Shrine|Q11665959|WD=Q11665959|1=Asuka-ni-Imasu Shrine}} (Asuka Village), becoming that shrine’s main sanctuary etc.

{{nihongo|'''Niukawakami Shrine Kamisha'''|丹生川上神社上社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Yamato Province]].

'''Niukawakami Shrine Upper Shrine''' (にうかわかみじんじゃかみしゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Sako, Kawakami Village, Yoshino District, Nara Prefecture. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Myojin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}) and a {{ill|lt=claim shrine|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}} of the {{ill|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines|Q215320|WD=Q215320|1=Twenty-Two Shrines}} (lower 8). Its former {{ill|lt=modern shrine rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was {{ill|lt=modern shrine rank system#Kan-sha|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Kanpei-taisha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}{{Efn2|However, that ranking was as a combined unit with the present Niukawakami Lower Shrine, or with all three including present Niukawakami Shrine (Middle Shrine).}}. Today it is a {{ill|lt=Beppyo shrine|Q10898274|WD=Q10898274|1=Beppyo shrine}} of {{ill|lt=Jinja Honchō|Q908077|WD=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}}.

The name distinguishes it from {{ill|lt=Niukawakami Shrine Lower Shrine|Q10877367|WD=Q10877367|1=Niukawakami Lower Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Shimoichi|Q1203064|WD=Q1203064|1=Shimoichi, Nara}}.

=== Enshrined deities ===
; Principal deity
* '''{{ill|lt=Okami-no-Kami|Q644201|WD=Q644201|lt=Takaokami-no-Kami|1=Kuraokami}}'''

; Accompanying deities
* {{ill|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi|Q386563|WD=Q386563|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi-no-Kami|1=Ōyamatsumi}}
* Ōikazuchi-no-Kami

Previously, {{ill|lt=Mizuhanome|Q8191796|WD=Q8191796|lt=Mizuhanome-no-Kami|1=Mizuhanome}} was principal deity, but when merged in {{ill|lt=1922|Q2165|WD=Q2165|1=1922}} (Taishō 11) with present {{ill|lt=Niukawakami Shrine|Q246370|WD=Q246370|lt=Niukawakami Shrine (Middle Shrine)|1=Niukawakami Shrine (Q246370)}}, it was changed to Takaokami-no-Kami. Takaokami-no-Kami has long been regarded as a deity of rain and rain-stopping.

=== History ===
Until early {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}, it was a small shrine called Takaokami Shrine, with unclear origin. Excavation with construction of {{ill|lt=Ōtaki Dam|Q11437563|WD=Q11437563|1=Ohtaki Dam}} discovered the Miyanotaira site{{Efn2|Early and late Jōmon pottery was found. Most stone tools were sinkers used in fishing nets, about 400 items.}}; directly under former main sanctuary remains were ritual-altar stones dating back before late Heian (late 11th century). Nearby were also found circular stone-arrangement remains with standing stones, seen as ritual remains from end of middle to early late {{ill|lt=Jōmon period|Q52824|WD=Q52824|1=Jōmon period}} (about 4,000 years ago). Though a break is recognized through Nara and Kofun periods, shrine ritual space may trace back to Jōmon.

In {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}} it was ranked {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Gōsha|1=UNKNOWN}}. But Etō Masazumi, assistant priest of then-Kanpei-taisha {{ill|lt=Niukawakami Shrine Lower Shrine|Q10877367|WD=Q10877367|lt=Niukawakami Shrine|1=Niukawakami Lower Shrine}} (present lower shrine), argued lower shrine location did not match the four-boundary area in the 895 Daijōkan decree ('{{ill|lt=Ruijū Sandai Kyaku|Q11665185|WD=Q11665185|1=Ruijū Sandai Kyaku}}'), identifying this shrine as the Engishiki Niukawakami Shrine. In 1874 this shrine became under lower shrine jurisdiction; lower shrine called “Kuchi-no-miya” and this shrine “Oku-no-miya.” After Etō’s view was accepted, in {{ill|lt=1896|Q7846|WD=Q7846|1=1896}} “Kuchi-no-miya” was renamed “Niukawakami Lower Shrine,” this shrine “Upper Shrine,” and both together became “Kanpei-taisha Niukawakami Shrine.”

However, in {{ill|lt=1915|Q2084|WD=Q2084|1=1915}} (Taishō 4), Moriguchi Narakichi of Higashiyoshino (site of present middle shrine) published 'Study of Niukawakami Shrine,' arguing “Aritōri Shrine (present middle shrine) = Niukawakami.” This was accepted, and by Interior Ministry notice of Oct. 12, 1922, middle shrine was designated; upper and lower shrines were incorporated into it, and all three again treated together as “Kanpei-taisha Niukawakami Shrine.” At that time, upper shrine deity changed back from Mizuhanome-no-Kami to Takaokami-no-Kami, as in its Gōsha period.

After World War II, in {{ill|lt=1952|Q5272|WD=Q5272|1=1952}} it became independent. It now belongs to {{ill|lt=Jinja Honchō|Q908077|WD=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}} and is a {{ill|lt=Beppyo shrine|Q10898274|WD=Q10898274|1=Beppyo shrine}}.

=== Precincts ===
Current main sanctuary is three-bay nagare-zukuri with copper roof. As old precinct area was to be submerged by Ōtaki Dam construction, it was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1998|Q2089|WD=Q2089|1=1998}} using old timber from former shrine buildings of {{ill|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|Q687168|WD=Q687168|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}. The old shrine buildings were built in {{ill|lt=1917|Q2092|WD=Q2092|1=1917}} and relocated to {{ill|lt=Asukaniimasu Shrine|Q11665959|WD=Q11665959|1=Asuka-ni-Imasu Shrine}} (Asuka Village), becoming that shrine’s main sanctuary etc.

{{nihongo|'''Niukawakami Shrine Kamisha'''|丹生川上神社上社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Yamato Province]].

'''Niukawakami Shrine Upper Shrine''' (にうかわかみじんじゃかみしゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Sako, Kawakami Village, Yoshino District, Nara Prefecture. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Myojin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}) and a {{ill|lt=claim shrine|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}} of the {{ill|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines|Q215320|WD=Q215320|1=Twenty-Two Shrines}} (lower 8). Its former {{ill|lt=modern shrine rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was {{ill|lt=modern shrine rank system#Kan-sha|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Kanpei-taisha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}{{Efn2|However, that ranking was as a combined unit with the present Niukawakami Lower Shrine, or with all three including present Niukawakami Shrine (Middle Shrine).}}. Today it is a {{ill|lt=Beppyo shrine|Q10898274|WD=Q10898274|1=Beppyo shrine}} of {{ill|lt=Jinja Honchō|Q908077|WD=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}}.

The name distinguishes it from {{ill|lt=Niukawakami Shrine Lower Shrine|Q10877367|WD=Q10877367|1=Niukawakami Lower Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Shimoichi|Q1203064|WD=Q1203064|1=Shimoichi, Nara}}.

=== Enshrined deities ===
; Principal deity
* '''{{ill|lt=Okami-no-Kami|Q644201|WD=Q644201|lt=Takaokami-no-Kami|1=Kuraokami}}'''

; Accompanying deities
* {{ill|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi|Q386563|WD=Q386563|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi-no-Kami|1=Ōyamatsumi}}
* Ōikazuchi-no-Kami

Previously, {{ill|lt=Mizuhanome|Q8191796|WD=Q8191796|lt=Mizuhanome-no-Kami|1=Mizuhanome}} was principal deity, but when merged in {{ill|lt=1922|Q2165|WD=Q2165|1=1922}} (Taishō 11) with present {{ill|lt=Niukawakami Shrine|Q246370|WD=Q246370|lt=Niukawakami Shrine (Middle Shrine)|1=Niukawakami Shrine (Q246370)}}, it was changed to Takaokami-no-Kami. Takaokami-no-Kami has long been regarded as a deity of rain and rain-stopping.

=== History ===
Until early {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}, it was a small shrine called Takaokami Shrine, with unclear origin. Excavation with construction of {{ill|lt=Ōtaki Dam|Q11437563|WD=Q11437563|1=Ohtaki Dam}} discovered the Miyanotaira site{{Efn2|Early and late Jōmon pottery was found. Most stone tools were sinkers used in fishing nets, about 400 items.}}; directly under former main sanctuary remains were ritual-altar stones dating back before late Heian (late 11th century). Nearby were also found circular stone-arrangement remains with standing stones, seen as ritual remains from end of middle to early late {{ill|lt=Jōmon period|Q52824|WD=Q52824|1=Jōmon period}} (about 4,000 years ago). Though a break is recognized through Nara and Kofun periods, shrine ritual space may trace back to Jōmon.

In {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}} it was ranked {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Gōsha|1=UNKNOWN}}. But Etō Masazumi, assistant priest of then-Kanpei-taisha {{ill|lt=Niukawakami Shrine Lower Shrine|Q10877367|WD=Q10877367|lt=Niukawakami Shrine|1=Niukawakami Lower Shrine}} (present lower shrine), argued lower shrine location did not match the four-boundary area in the 895 Daijōkan decree ('{{ill|lt=Ruijū Sandai Kyaku|Q11665185|WD=Q11665185|1=Ruijū Sandai Kyaku}}'), identifying this shrine as the Engishiki Niukawakami Shrine. In 1874 this shrine became under lower shrine jurisdiction; lower shrine called “Kuchi-no-miya” and this shrine “Oku-no-miya.” After Etō’s view was accepted, in {{ill|lt=1896|Q7846|WD=Q7846|1=1896}} “Kuchi-no-miya” was renamed “Niukawakami Lower Shrine,” this shrine “Upper Shrine,” and both together became “Kanpei-taisha Niukawakami Shrine.”

However, in {{ill|lt=1915|Q2084|WD=Q2084|1=1915}} (Taishō 4), Moriguchi Narakichi of Higashiyoshino (site of present middle shrine) published 'Study of Niukawakami Shrine,' arguing “Aritōri Shrine (present middle shrine) = Niukawakami.” This was accepted, and by Interior Ministry notice of Oct. 12, 1922, middle shrine was designated; upper and lower shrines were incorporated into it, and all three again treated together as “Kanpei-taisha Niukawakami Shrine.” At that time, upper shrine deity changed back from Mizuhanome-no-Kami to Takaokami-no-Kami, as in its Gōsha period.

After World War II, in {{ill|lt=1952|Q5272|WD=Q5272|1=1952}} it became independent. It now belongs to {{ill|lt=Jinja Honchō|Q908077|WD=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}} and is a {{ill|lt=Beppyo shrine|Q10898274|WD=Q10898274|1=Beppyo shrine}}.

=== Precincts ===
Current main sanctuary is three-bay nagare-zukuri with copper roof. As old precinct area was to be submerged by Ōtaki Dam construction, it was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1998|Q2089|WD=Q2089|1=1998}} using old timber from former shrine buildings of {{ill|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|Q687168|WD=Q687168|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}. The old shrine buildings were built in {{ill|lt=1917|Q2092|WD=Q2092|1=1917}} and relocated to {{ill|lt=Asukaniimasu Shrine|Q11665959|WD=Q11665959|1=Asuka-ni-Imasu Shrine}} (Asuka Village), becoming that shrine’s main sanctuary etc.

{{nihongo|'''Niukawakami Shrine Kamisha'''|丹生川上神社上社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Yamato Province]].

'''Niukawakami Shrine Upper Shrine''' (にうかわかみじんじゃかみしゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Sako, Kawakami Village, Yoshino District, Nara Prefecture. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Myojin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}) and a {{ill|lt=claim shrine|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}} of the {{ill|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines|Q215320|WD=Q215320|1=Twenty-Two Shrines}} (lower 8). Its former {{ill|lt=modern shrine rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was {{ill|lt=modern shrine rank system#Kan-sha|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Kanpei-taisha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}{{Efn2|However, that ranking was as a combined unit with the present Niukawakami Lower Shrine, or with all three including present Niukawakami Shrine (Middle Shrine).}}. Today it is a {{ill|lt=Beppyo shrine|Q10898274|WD=Q10898274|1=Beppyo shrine}} of {{ill|lt=Jinja Honchō|Q908077|WD=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}}.

The name distinguishes it from {{ill|lt=Niukawakami Shrine Lower Shrine|Q10877367|WD=Q10877367|1=Niukawakami Lower Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Shimoichi|Q1203064|WD=Q1203064|1=Shimoichi, Nara}}.

=== Enshrined deities ===
; Principal deity
* '''{{ill|lt=Okami-no-Kami|Q644201|WD=Q644201|lt=Takaokami-no-Kami|1=Kuraokami}}'''

; Accompanying deities
* {{ill|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi|Q386563|WD=Q386563|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi-no-Kami|1=Ōyamatsumi}}
* Ōikazuchi-no-Kami

Previously, {{ill|lt=Mizuhanome|Q8191796|WD=Q8191796|lt=Mizuhanome-no-Kami|1=Mizuhanome}} was principal deity, but when merged in {{ill|lt=1922|Q2165|WD=Q2165|1=1922}} (Taishō 11) with present {{ill|lt=Niukawakami Shrine|Q246370|WD=Q246370|lt=Niukawakami Shrine (Middle Shrine)|1=Niukawakami Shrine (Q246370)}}, it was changed to Takaokami-no-Kami. Takaokami-no-Kami has long been regarded as a deity of rain and rain-stopping.

=== History ===
Until early {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}, it was a small shrine called Takaokami Shrine, with unclear origin. Excavation with construction of {{ill|lt=Ōtaki Dam|Q11437563|WD=Q11437563|1=Ohtaki Dam}} discovered the Miyanotaira site{{Efn2|Early and late Jōmon pottery was found. Most stone tools were sinkers used in fishing nets, about 400 items.}}; directly under former main sanctuary remains were ritual-altar stones dating back before late Heian (late 11th century). Nearby were also found circular stone-arrangement remains with standing stones, seen as ritual remains from end of middle to early late {{ill|lt=Jōmon period|Q52824|WD=Q52824|1=Jōmon period}} (about 4,000 years ago). Though a break is recognized through Nara and Kofun periods, shrine ritual space may trace back to Jōmon.

In {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}} it was ranked {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Gōsha|1=UNKNOWN}}. But Etō Masazumi, assistant priest of then-Kanpei-taisha {{ill|lt=Niukawakami Shrine Lower Shrine|Q10877367|WD=Q10877367|lt=Niukawakami Shrine|1=Niukawakami Lower Shrine}} (present lower shrine), argued lower shrine location did not match the four-boundary area in the 895 Daijōkan decree ('{{ill|lt=Ruijū Sandai Kyaku|Q11665185|WD=Q11665185|1=Ruijū Sandai Kyaku}}'), identifying this shrine as the Engishiki Niukawakami Shrine. In 1874 this shrine became under lower shrine jurisdiction; lower shrine called “Kuchi-no-miya” and this shrine “Oku-no-miya.” After Etō’s view was accepted, in {{ill|lt=1896|Q7846|WD=Q7846|1=1896}} “Kuchi-no-miya” was renamed “Niukawakami Lower Shrine,” this shrine “Upper Shrine,” and both together became “Kanpei-taisha Niukawakami Shrine.”

However, in {{ill|lt=1915|Q2084|WD=Q2084|1=1915}} (Taishō 4), Moriguchi Narakichi of Higashiyoshino (site of present middle shrine) published 'Study of Niukawakami Shrine,' arguing “Aritōri Shrine (present middle shrine) = Niukawakami.” This was accepted, and by Interior Ministry notice of Oct. 12, 1922, middle shrine was designated; upper and lower shrines were incorporated into it, and all three again treated together as “Kanpei-taisha Niukawakami Shrine.” At that time, upper shrine deity changed back from Mizuhanome-no-Kami to Takaokami-no-Kami, as in its Gōsha period.

After World War II, in {{ill|lt=1952|Q5272|WD=Q5272|1=1952}} it became independent. It now belongs to {{ill|lt=Jinja Honchō|Q908077|WD=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}} and is a {{ill|lt=Beppyo shrine|Q10898274|WD=Q10898274|1=Beppyo shrine}}.

=== Precincts ===
Current main sanctuary is three-bay nagare-zukuri with copper roof. As old precinct area was to be submerged by Ōtaki Dam construction, it was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1998|Q2089|WD=Q2089|1=1998}} using old timber from former shrine buildings of {{ill|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|Q687168|WD=Q687168|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}. The old shrine buildings were built in {{ill|lt=1917|Q2092|WD=Q2092|1=1917}} and relocated to {{ill|lt=Asukaniimasu Shrine|Q11665959|WD=Q11665959|1=Asuka-ni-Imasu Shrine}} (Asuka Village), becoming that shrine’s main sanctuary etc.

{{nihongo|'''Niukawakami Shrine Kamisha'''|丹生川上神社上社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Yamato Province]].

'''Niukawakami Shrine Upper Shrine''' (にうかわかみじんじゃかみしゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Sako, Kawakami Village, Yoshino District, Nara Prefecture. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Myojin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}) and a {{ill|lt=claim shrine|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}} of the {{ill|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines|Q215320|WD=Q215320|1=Twenty-Two Shrines}} (lower 8). Its former {{ill|lt=modern shrine rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was {{ill|lt=modern shrine rank system#Kan-sha|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Kanpei-taisha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}{{Efn2|However, that ranking was as a combined unit with the present Niukawakami Lower Shrine, or with all three including present Niukawakami Shrine (Middle Shrine).}}. Today it is a {{ill|lt=Beppyo shrine|Q10898274|WD=Q10898274|1=Beppyo shrine}} of {{ill|lt=Jinja Honchō|Q908077|WD=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}}.

The name distinguishes it from {{ill|lt=Niukawakami Shrine Lower Shrine|Q10877367|WD=Q10877367|1=Niukawakami Lower Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Shimoichi|Q1203064|WD=Q1203064|1=Shimoichi, Nara}}.

=== Enshrined deities ===
; Principal deity
* '''{{ill|lt=Okami-no-Kami|Q644201|WD=Q644201|lt=Takaokami-no-Kami|1=Kuraokami}}'''

; Accompanying deities
* {{ill|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi|Q386563|WD=Q386563|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi-no-Kami|1=Ōyamatsumi}}
* Ōikazuchi-no-Kami

Previously, {{ill|lt=Mizuhanome|Q8191796|WD=Q8191796|lt=Mizuhanome-no-Kami|1=Mizuhanome}} was principal deity, but when merged in {{ill|lt=1922|Q2165|WD=Q2165|1=1922}} (Taishō 11) with present {{ill|lt=Niukawakami Shrine|Q246370|WD=Q246370|lt=Niukawakami Shrine (Middle Shrine)|1=Niukawakami Shrine (Q246370)}}, it was changed to Takaokami-no-Kami. Takaokami-no-Kami has long been regarded as a deity of rain and rain-stopping.

=== History ===
Until early {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}, it was a small shrine called Takaokami Shrine, with unclear origin. Excavation with construction of {{ill|lt=Ōtaki Dam|Q11437563|WD=Q11437563|1=Ohtaki Dam}} discovered the Miyanotaira site{{Efn2|Early and late Jōmon pottery was found. Most stone tools were sinkers used in fishing nets, about 400 items.}}; directly under former main sanctuary remains were ritual-altar stones dating back before late Heian (late 11th century). Nearby were also found circular stone-arrangement remains with standing stones, seen as ritual remains from end of middle to early late {{ill|lt=Jōmon period|Q52824|WD=Q52824|1=Jōmon period}} (about 4,000 years ago). Though a break is recognized through Nara and Kofun periods, shrine ritual space may trace back to Jōmon.

In {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}} it was ranked {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Gōsha|1=UNKNOWN}}. But Etō Masazumi, assistant priest of then-Kanpei-taisha {{ill|lt=Niukawakami Shrine Lower Shrine|Q10877367|WD=Q10877367|lt=Niukawakami Shrine|1=Niukawakami Lower Shrine}} (present lower shrine), argued lower shrine location did not match the four-boundary area in the 895 Daijōkan decree ('{{ill|lt=Ruijū Sandai Kyaku|Q11665185|WD=Q11665185|1=Ruijū Sandai Kyaku}}'), identifying this shrine as the Engishiki Niukawakami Shrine. In 1874 this shrine became under lower shrine jurisdiction; lower shrine called “Kuchi-no-miya” and this shrine “Oku-no-miya.” After Etō’s view was accepted, in {{ill|lt=1896|Q7846|WD=Q7846|1=1896}} “Kuchi-no-miya” was renamed “Niukawakami Lower Shrine,” this shrine “Upper Shrine,” and both together became “Kanpei-taisha Niukawakami Shrine.”

However, in {{ill|lt=1915|Q2084|WD=Q2084|1=1915}} (Taishō 4), Moriguchi Narakichi of Higashiyoshino (site of present middle shrine) published 'Study of Niukawakami Shrine,' arguing “Aritōri Shrine (present middle shrine) = Niukawakami.” This was accepted, and by Interior Ministry notice of Oct. 12, 1922, middle shrine was designated; upper and lower shrines were incorporated into it, and all three again treated together as “Kanpei-taisha Niukawakami Shrine.” At that time, upper shrine deity changed back from Mizuhanome-no-Kami to Takaokami-no-Kami, as in its Gōsha period.

After World War II, in {{ill|lt=1952|Q5272|WD=Q5272|1=1952}} it became independent. It now belongs to {{ill|lt=Jinja Honchō|Q908077|WD=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}} and is a {{ill|lt=Beppyo shrine|Q10898274|WD=Q10898274|1=Beppyo shrine}}.

=== Precincts ===
Current main sanctuary is three-bay nagare-zukuri with copper roof. As old precinct area was to be submerged by Ōtaki Dam construction, it was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1998|Q2089|WD=Q2089|1=1998}} using old timber from former shrine buildings of {{ill|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|Q687168|WD=Q687168|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}. The old shrine buildings were built in {{ill|lt=1917|Q2092|WD=Q2092|1=1917}} and relocated to {{ill|lt=Asukaniimasu Shrine|Q11665959|WD=Q11665959|1=Asuka-ni-Imasu Shrine}} (Asuka Village), becoming that shrine’s main sanctuary etc.

{{nihongo|'''Niukawakami Shrine Kamisha'''|丹生川上神社上社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Yamato Province]].

'''Niukawakami Shrine Upper Shrine''' (にうかわかみじんじゃかみしゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Sako, Kawakami Village, Yoshino District, Nara Prefecture. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Myojin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}) and a {{ill|lt=claim shrine|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}} of the {{ill|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines|Q215320|WD=Q215320|1=Twenty-Two Shrines}} (lower 8). Its former {{ill|lt=modern shrine rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was {{ill|lt=modern shrine rank system#Kan-sha|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Kanpei-taisha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}{{Efn2|However, that ranking was as a combined unit with the present Niukawakami Lower Shrine, or with all three including present Niukawakami Shrine (Middle Shrine).}}. Today it is a {{ill|lt=Beppyo shrine|Q10898274|WD=Q10898274|1=Beppyo shrine}} of {{ill|lt=Jinja Honchō|Q908077|WD=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}}.

The name distinguishes it from {{ill|lt=Niukawakami Shrine Lower Shrine|Q10877367|WD=Q10877367|1=Niukawakami Lower Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Shimoichi|Q1203064|WD=Q1203064|1=Shimoichi, Nara}}.

=== Enshrined deities ===
; Principal deity
* '''{{ill|lt=Okami-no-Kami|Q644201|WD=Q644201|lt=Takaokami-no-Kami|1=Kuraokami}}'''

; Accompanying deities
* {{ill|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi|Q386563|WD=Q386563|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi-no-Kami|1=Ōyamatsumi}}
* Ōikazuchi-no-Kami

Previously, {{ill|lt=Mizuhanome|Q8191796|WD=Q8191796|lt=Mizuhanome-no-Kami|1=Mizuhanome}} was principal deity, but when merged in {{ill|lt=1922|Q2165|WD=Q2165|1=1922}} (Taishō 11) with present {{ill|lt=Niukawakami Shrine|Q246370|WD=Q246370|lt=Niukawakami Shrine (Middle Shrine)|1=Niukawakami Shrine (Q246370)}}, it was changed to Takaokami-no-Kami. Takaokami-no-Kami has long been regarded as a deity of rain and rain-stopping.

=== History ===
Until early {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}, it was a small shrine called Takaokami Shrine, with unclear origin. Excavation with construction of {{ill|lt=Ōtaki Dam|Q11437563|WD=Q11437563|1=Ohtaki Dam}} discovered the Miyanotaira site{{Efn2|Early and late Jōmon pottery was found. Most stone tools were sinkers used in fishing nets, about 400 items.}}; directly under former main sanctuary remains were ritual-altar stones dating back before late Heian (late 11th century). Nearby were also found circular stone-arrangement remains with standing stones, seen as ritual remains from end of middle to early late {{ill|lt=Jōmon period|Q52824|WD=Q52824|1=Jōmon period}} (about 4,000 years ago). Though a break is recognized through Nara and Kofun periods, shrine ritual space may trace back to Jōmon.

In {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}} it was ranked {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Gōsha|1=UNKNOWN}}. But Etō Masazumi, assistant priest of then-Kanpei-taisha {{ill|lt=Niukawakami Shrine Lower Shrine|Q10877367|WD=Q10877367|lt=Niukawakami Shrine|1=Niukawakami Lower Shrine}} (present lower shrine), argued lower shrine location did not match the four-boundary area in the 895 Daijōkan decree ('{{ill|lt=Ruijū Sandai Kyaku|Q11665185|WD=Q11665185|1=Ruijū Sandai Kyaku}}'), identifying this shrine as the Engishiki Niukawakami Shrine. In 1874 this shrine became under lower shrine jurisdiction; lower shrine called “Kuchi-no-miya” and this shrine “Oku-no-miya.” After Etō’s view was accepted, in {{ill|lt=1896|Q7846|WD=Q7846|1=1896}} “Kuchi-no-miya” was renamed “Niukawakami Lower Shrine,” this shrine “Upper Shrine,” and both together became “Kanpei-taisha Niukawakami Shrine.”

However, in {{ill|lt=1915|Q2084|WD=Q2084|1=1915}} (Taishō 4), Moriguchi Narakichi of Higashiyoshino (site of present middle shrine) published 'Study of Niukawakami Shrine,' arguing “Aritōri Shrine (present middle shrine) = Niukawakami.” This was accepted, and by Interior Ministry notice of Oct. 12, 1922, middle shrine was designated; upper and lower shrines were incorporated into it, and all three again treated together as “Kanpei-taisha Niukawakami Shrine.” At that time, upper shrine deity changed back from Mizuhanome-no-Kami to Takaokami-no-Kami, as in its Gōsha period.

After World War II, in {{ill|lt=1952|Q5272|WD=Q5272|1=1952}} it became independent. It now belongs to {{ill|lt=Jinja Honchō|Q908077|WD=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}} and is a {{ill|lt=Beppyo shrine|Q10898274|WD=Q10898274|1=Beppyo shrine}}.

=== Precincts ===
Current main sanctuary is three-bay nagare-zukuri with copper roof. As old precinct area was to be submerged by Ōtaki Dam construction, it was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1998|Q2089|WD=Q2089|1=1998}} using old timber from former shrine buildings of {{ill|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|Q687168|WD=Q687168|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}. The old shrine buildings were built in {{ill|lt=1917|Q2092|WD=Q2092|1=1917}} and relocated to {{ill|lt=Asukaniimasu Shrine|Q11665959|WD=Q11665959|1=Asuka-ni-Imasu Shrine}} (Asuka Village), becoming that shrine’s main sanctuary etc.

{{nihongo|'''Niukawakami Shrine Kamisha'''|丹生川上神社上社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Yamato Province]].

'''Niukawakami Shrine Upper Shrine''' (にうかわかみじんじゃかみしゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Sako, Kawakami Village, Yoshino District, Nara Prefecture. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Myojin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}) and a {{ill|lt=claim shrine|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}} of the {{ill|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines|Q215320|WD=Q215320|1=Twenty-Two Shrines}} (lower 8). Its former {{ill|lt=modern shrine rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was {{ill|lt=modern shrine rank system#Kan-sha|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Kanpei-taisha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}{{Efn2|However, that ranking was as a combined unit with the present Niukawakami Lower Shrine, or with all three including present Niukawakami Shrine (Middle Shrine).}}. Today it is a {{ill|lt=Beppyo shrine|Q10898274|WD=Q10898274|1=Beppyo shrine}} of {{ill|lt=Jinja Honchō|Q908077|WD=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}}.

The name distinguishes it from {{ill|lt=Niukawakami Shrine Lower Shrine|Q10877367|WD=Q10877367|1=Niukawakami Lower Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Shimoichi|Q1203064|WD=Q1203064|1=Shimoichi, Nara}}.

=== Enshrined deities ===
; Principal deity
* '''{{ill|lt=Okami-no-Kami|Q644201|WD=Q644201|lt=Takaokami-no-Kami|1=Kuraokami}}'''

; Accompanying deities
* {{ill|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi|Q386563|WD=Q386563|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi-no-Kami|1=Ōyamatsumi}}
* Ōikazuchi-no-Kami

Previously, {{ill|lt=Mizuhanome|Q8191796|WD=Q8191796|lt=Mizuhanome-no-Kami|1=Mizuhanome}} was principal deity, but when merged in {{ill|lt=1922|Q2165|WD=Q2165|1=1922}} (Taishō 11) with present {{ill|lt=Niukawakami Shrine|Q246370|WD=Q246370|lt=Niukawakami Shrine (Middle Shrine)|1=Niukawakami Shrine (Q246370)}}, it was changed to Takaokami-no-Kami. Takaokami-no-Kami has long been regarded as a deity of rain and rain-stopping.

=== History ===
Until early {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}, it was a small shrine called Takaokami Shrine, with unclear origin. Excavation with construction of {{ill|lt=Ōtaki Dam|Q11437563|WD=Q11437563|1=Ohtaki Dam}} discovered the Miyanotaira site{{Efn2|Early and late Jōmon pottery was found. Most stone tools were sinkers used in fishing nets, about 400 items.}}; directly under former main sanctuary remains were ritual-altar stones dating back before late Heian (late 11th century). Nearby were also found circular stone-arrangement remains with standing stones, seen as ritual remains from end of middle to early late {{ill|lt=Jōmon period|Q52824|WD=Q52824|1=Jōmon period}} (about 4,000 years ago). Though a break is recognized through Nara and Kofun periods, shrine ritual space may trace back to Jōmon.

In {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}} it was ranked {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Gōsha|1=UNKNOWN}}. But Etō Masazumi, assistant priest of then-Kanpei-taisha {{ill|lt=Niukawakami Shrine Lower Shrine|Q10877367|WD=Q10877367|lt=Niukawakami Shrine|1=Niukawakami Lower Shrine}} (present lower shrine), argued lower shrine location did not match the four-boundary area in the 895 Daijōkan decree ('{{ill|lt=Ruijū Sandai Kyaku|Q11665185|WD=Q11665185|1=Ruijū Sandai Kyaku}}'), identifying this shrine as the Engishiki Niukawakami Shrine. In 1874 this shrine became under lower shrine jurisdiction; lower shrine called “Kuchi-no-miya” and this shrine “Oku-no-miya.” After Etō’s view was accepted, in {{ill|lt=1896|Q7846|WD=Q7846|1=1896}} “Kuchi-no-miya” was renamed “Niukawakami Lower Shrine,” this shrine “Upper Shrine,” and both together became “Kanpei-taisha Niukawakami Shrine.”

However, in {{ill|lt=1915|Q2084|WD=Q2084|1=1915}} (Taishō 4), Moriguchi Narakichi of Higashiyoshino (site of present middle shrine) published 'Study of Niukawakami Shrine,' arguing “Aritōri Shrine (present middle shrine) = Niukawakami.” This was accepted, and by Interior Ministry notice of Oct. 12, 1922, middle shrine was designated; upper and lower shrines were incorporated into it, and all three again treated together as “Kanpei-taisha Niukawakami Shrine.” At that time, upper shrine deity changed back from Mizuhanome-no-Kami to Takaokami-no-Kami, as in its Gōsha period.

After World War II, in {{ill|lt=1952|Q5272|WD=Q5272|1=1952}} it became independent. It now belongs to {{ill|lt=Jinja Honchō|Q908077|WD=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}} and is a {{ill|lt=Beppyo shrine|Q10898274|WD=Q10898274|1=Beppyo shrine}}.

=== Precincts ===
Current main sanctuary is three-bay nagare-zukuri with copper roof. As old precinct area was to be submerged by Ōtaki Dam construction, it was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1998|Q2089|WD=Q2089|1=1998}} using old timber from former shrine buildings of {{ill|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|Q687168|WD=Q687168|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}. The old shrine buildings were built in {{ill|lt=1917|Q2092|WD=Q2092|1=1917}} and relocated to {{ill|lt=Asukaniimasu Shrine|Q11665959|WD=Q11665959|1=Asuka-ni-Imasu Shrine}} (Asuka Village), becoming that shrine’s main sanctuary etc.

File:Niukawakami-jinja Kamisha Keidai 001.jpg|View from area in front of worship hall

* Suijin-sha (Mitsuhanome-no-Mikoto)

{{nihongo|'''Niukawakami Shrine Kamisha'''|丹生川上神社上社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Yamato Province]].

'''Niukawakami Shrine Upper Shrine''' (にうかわかみじんじゃかみしゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Sako, Kawakami Village, Yoshino District, Nara Prefecture. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Myojin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}) and a {{ill|lt=claim shrine|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}} of the {{ill|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines|Q215320|WD=Q215320|1=Twenty-Two Shrines}} (lower 8). Its former {{ill|lt=modern shrine rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was {{ill|lt=modern shrine rank system#Kan-sha|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=Kanpei-taisha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}{{Efn2|However, that ranking was as a combined unit with the present Niukawakami Lower Shrine, or with all three including present Niukawakami Shrine (Middle Shrine).}}. Today it is a {{ill|lt=Beppyo shrine|Q10898274|WD=Q10898274|1=Beppyo shrine}} of {{ill|lt=Jinja Honchō|Q908077|WD=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}}.

The name distinguishes it from {{ill|lt=Niukawakami Shrine Lower Shrine|Q10877367|WD=Q10877367|1=Niukawakami Lower Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Shimoichi|Q1203064|WD=Q1203064|1=Shimoichi, Nara}}.

=== Enshrined deities ===
; Principal deity
* '''{{ill|lt=Okami-no-Kami|Q644201|WD=Q644201|lt=Takaokami-no-Kami|1=Kuraokami}}'''

; Accompanying deities
* {{ill|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi|Q386563|WD=Q386563|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi-no-Kami|1=Ōyamatsumi}}
* Ōikazuchi-no-Kami

Previously, {{ill|lt=Mizuhanome|Q8191796|WD=Q8191796|lt=Mizuhanome-no-Kami|1=Mizuhanome}} was principal deity, but when merged in {{ill|lt=1922|Q2165|WD=Q2165|1=1922}} (Taishō 11) with present {{ill|lt=Niukawakami Shrine|Q246370|WD=Q246370|lt=Niukawakami Shrine (Middle Shrine)|1=Niukawakami Shrine (Q246370)}}, it was changed to Takaokami-no-Kami. Takaokami-no-Kami has long been regarded as a deity of rain and rain-stopping.

=== History ===
Until early {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}, it was a small shrine called Takaokami Shrine, with unclear origin. Excavation with construction of {{ill|lt=Ōtaki Dam|Q11437563|WD=Q11437563|1=Ohtaki Dam}} discovered the Miyanotaira site{{Efn2|Early and late Jōmon pottery was found. Most stone tools were sinkers used in fishing nets, about 400 items.}}; directly under former main sanctuary remains were ritual-altar stones dating back before late Heian (late 11th century). Nearby were also found circular stone-arrangement remains with standing stones, seen as ritual remains from end of middle to early late {{ill|lt=Jōmon period|Q52824|WD=Q52824|1=Jōmon period}} (about 4,000 years ago). Though a break is recognized through Nara and Kofun periods, shrine ritual space may trace back to Jōmon.

In {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}} it was ranked {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Gōsha|1=UNKNOWN}}. But Etō Masazumi, assistant priest of then-Kanpei-taisha {{ill|lt=Niukawakami Shrine Lower Shrine|Q10877367|WD=Q10877367|lt=Niukawakami Shrine|1=Niukawakami Lower Shrine}} (present lower shrine), argued lower shrine location did not match the four-boundary area in the 895 Daijōkan decree ('{{ill|lt=Ruijū Sandai Kyaku|Q11665185|WD=Q11665185|1=Ruijū Sandai Kyaku}}'), identifying this shrine as the Engishiki Niukawakami Shrine. In 1874 this shrine became under lower shrine jurisdiction; lower shrine called “Kuchi-no-miya” and this shrine “Oku-no-miya.” After Etō’s view was accepted, in {{ill|lt=1896|Q7846|WD=Q7846|1=1896}} “Kuchi-no-miya” was renamed “Niukawakami Lower Shrine,” this shrine “Upper Shrine,” and both together became “Kanpei-taisha Niukawakami Shrine.”

However, in {{ill|lt=1915|Q2084|WD=Q2084|1=1915}} (Taishō 4), Moriguchi Narakichi of Higashiyoshino (site of present middle shrine) published 'Study of Niukawakami Shrine,' arguing “Aritōri Shrine (present middle shrine) = Niukawakami.” This was accepted, and by Interior Ministry notice of Oct. 12, 1922, middle shrine was designated; upper and lower shrines were incorporated into it, and all three again treated together as “Kanpei-taisha Niukawakami Shrine.” At that time, upper shrine deity changed back from Mizuhanome-no-Kami to Takaokami-no-Kami, as in its Gōsha period.

After World War II, in {{ill|lt=1952|Q5272|WD=Q5272|1=1952}} it became independent. It now belongs to {{ill|lt=Jinja Honchō|Q908077|WD=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}} and is a {{ill|lt=Beppyo shrine|Q10898274|WD=Q10898274|1=Beppyo shrine}}.

=== Precincts ===
Current main sanctuary is three-bay nagare-zukuri with copper roof. As old precinct area was to be submerged by Ōtaki Dam construction, it was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1998|Q2089|WD=Q2089|1=1998}} using old timber from former shrine buildings of {{ill|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|Q687168|WD=Q687168|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}. The old shrine buildings were built in {{ill|lt=1917|Q2092|WD=Q2092|1=1917}} and relocated to {{ill|lt=Asukaniimasu Shrine|Q11665959|WD=Q11665959|1=Asuka-ni-Imasu Shrine}} (Asuka Village), becoming that shrine’s main sanctuary etc.
